prePostQuiz.explanation: A HAGL lesion is humeral-sided avulsion of the inferior glenohumeral ligament, producing a 'J-sign' of the axillary pouch and an extra-articular contrast leak on the humeral side, with the glenoid labrum intact. A Bankart and a Perthes are glenoid/labral-sided lesions and a GLAD is a glenolabral cartilage injury, so all three represent the classic pitfall of fixating on the glenoid side while missing the humeral-sided ligament avulsion; none produces a humeral contrast leak or a J-shaped pouch.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 26-year-old has had four anterior dislocations. MRI suggests substantial anterior glenoid bone loss and a Hill-Sachs lesion that appears to engage. The surgeon wants precise osseous quantification to decide between an arthroscopic soft-tissue repair and a Latarjet. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed to arthroscopic Bankart repair on clinical grounds alone
Obtain a radionuclide bone scan of the glenoid rim
Repeat MRI with intra-articular gadolinium to re-image the labrum
Obtain CT to quantify glenoid bone loss and the Hill-Sachs track

prePostQuiz.explanation: When MRI suggests significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20% or more) or an engaging/off-track Hill-Sachs, CT — often 3D with the humeral head digitally subtracted — best quantifies the osseous defect and drives the choice between soft-tissue repair and a bony procedure such as Latarjet. Proceeding to isolated Bankart repair ignores that high bone loss predicts soft-tissue-repair failure, a bone scan gives no quantification of bone loss, and an MR arthrogram characterizes soft tissue but underestimates osseous defects compared with CT.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 19-year-old collision-sport athlete has recurrent anterior dislocations. CT shows ~25% anterior glenoid bone loss and an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion. Based on these osseous findings, which surgical strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Isolated arthroscopic Bankart repair, the labrum being the primary stabilizer
Nonoperative management with bracing and activity modification
A bony augmentation procedure such as Latarjet to restore the deficient glenoid arc
Arthroscopic capsular plication to retension the anterior capsule

prePostQuiz.explanation: Critical glenoid bone loss (around 20-25% or more) with an off-track/engaging Hill-Sachs predicts high failure rates for soft-tissue repair, so a bony procedure such as Latarjet — which lengthens the glenoid arc and adds a sling effect — is favored. Isolated Bankart repair reflects the misconception that labral repair suffices despite bone loss, bracing wrongly assumes bone loss is irrelevant to recurrence, and capsular plication addresses soft tissue but does not restore the deficient bony track.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 54-year-old with diabetes has months of progressive global stiffness and a marked loss of passive external rotation. Which MRI finding most specifically supports adhesive capsulitis rather than a rotator cuff tear?

options.text: Fluid-bright signal spanning the supraspinatus from articular to bursal surface
Thickening and edema of the coracohumeral ligament and rotator interval with obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle
Intermediate signal and thickening of the supraspinatus tendon without a surface defect
Fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa

prePostQuiz.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis shows soft-tissue thickening and edema at the rotator interval and coracohumeral ligament with loss of the normal subcoracoid fat triangle, plus axillary pouch/inferior capsule thickening — findings that explain the global loss of motion. Fluid-bright transmural supraspinatus signal describes a full-thickness cuff tear (the mimic to be excluded), intermediate non-surfacing tendon signal describes tendinosis, and bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not indicate capsulitis.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 49-year-old has 4 months of painful shoulder stiffness limiting motion in all planes. MRI is ordered to distinguish adhesive capsulitis from a cuff problem. Which constellation of findings is most specific for adhesive capsulitis?

options.text: Retraction of the supraspinatus to the glenoid with Goutallier grade 3 fatty infiltration
A laterally extending superior labral contrast cleft with irregular margins
Thickening and edema of the axillary pouch and inferior glenohumeral ligament with rotator-interval and coracohumeral ligament thickening
Isolated subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid without capsular changes

prePostQuiz.explanation: The specific MRI signature of adhesive capsulitis is thickening and edema of the axillary pouch/inferior glenohumeral ligament combined with rotator-interval and coracohumeral ligament thickening and obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle, often with capsular enhancement. Supraspinatus retraction with fatty infiltration describes a chronic cuff tear (the mimic), a laterally extending irregular superior labral cleft describes a SLAP tear, and isolated bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not establish capsulitis.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 35-year-old volleyball player has posterior shoulder weakness. MRI shows a posterosuperior labral tear with a paralabral cyst extending into the spinoglenoid notch. Which additional finding best explains the weakness and should be reported?

options.text: Denervation of both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, from compression at the suprascapular notch
Denervation of the deltoid, from axillary nerve compression in the quadrilateral space
Denervation edema or fatty atrophy of the infraspinatus alone, from suprascapular nerve compression at the spinoglenoid notch
Denervation of the subscapularis, from upper subscapular nerve involvement

prePostQuiz.explanation: A cyst at the spinoglenoid notch compresses the suprascapular nerve after its branch to the supraspinatus has departed, so denervation is isolated to the infraspinatus (edema acutely, fatty atrophy chronically). Denervation of both muscles is the more proximal suprascapular-notch pattern, and deltoid or subscapularis denervation involves different nerves (axillary, subscapular) not affected by a spinoglenoid notch cyst; the teaching point is to look beyond the labrum to muscle denervation signal.

prePostQuiz.stem: A 41-year-old has shoulder pain and weakness of both abduction and external rotation. MRI shows a paralabral cyst arising from a superior labral tear that extends medially into the suprascapular notch. Which pattern of muscle denervation do you most expect?

options.text: Isolated infraspinatus denervation, the cyst lying distal to the supraspinatus branch
Isolated teres minor denervation, from axillary nerve compression
Isolated supraspinatus denervation, the cyst sparing the infraspinatus branch
Denervation of both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, the cyst compressing the suprascapular nerve at the suprascapular notch

prePostQuiz.explanation: A cyst at the suprascapular notch compresses the nerve proximal to both motor branches, so both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus show denervation, matching the combined abduction and external-rotation weakness. Isolated infraspinatus denervation is the more distal spinoglenoid-notch pattern, isolated supraspinatus denervation inverts the anatomy because a proximal lesion cannot spare the infraspinatus, and teres minor denervation involves a different nerve; the key is to localize the cyst along the nerve's course.

prePostQuiz.stem: Five months after supraspinatus repair, a patient has persistent pain. On fluid-sensitive images the repaired tendon shows intermediate-to-heterogeneous signal and mild thickening, with a little subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid and focal susceptibility artifact near the anchors. How should this be interpreted?

options.text: Recurrent full-thickness tear, because persistent intratendinous signal indicates failure
Recurrent full-thickness tear, because subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid confirms a gap
Recurrent full-thickness tear, because anchor susceptibility artifact represents a tendon defect
Expected postoperative change, because heterogeneous signal, mild bursal fluid, and anchor artifact are normal here

prePostQuiz.explanation: Repaired cuff tendons normally remain thickened and heterogeneous with intermediate-to-increased signal for many months, and mild bursal fluid and anchor susceptibility artifact are expected, so none of these indicates failure. Options A, B, and C each reflect the common error of overcalling a recurrent tear from nonspecific postoperative signal, reactive bursal fluid, or hardware artifact; a recurrent full-thickness tear instead requires a fluid-bright gap spanning the tendon with fluid communication.

prePostQuiz.stem: Seven months after rotator cuff repair, a patient has recurrent pain and weakness. On fluid-sensitive fat-saturated images there is a fluid-bright (fluid-equivalent) defect spanning the full thickness of the repaired tendon, with fluid communicating between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: Recurrent full-thickness tear, because a fluid-bright gap spans the tendon with joint-to-bursa communication
Expected postoperative change, because some increased intratendinous signal is normal after repair
Expected postoperative change, because anchor susceptibility artifact mimics a defect
Tendinosis of the repaired tendon, because the signal is intermediate rather than fluid-bright

prePostQuiz.explanation: A fluid-bright (fluid-equivalent) defect spanning the full tendon thickness with fluid communication between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa is the reliable sign of a recurrent full-thickness tear. Options B and C reflect the opposite error of dismissing a true fluid-bright gap as benign postoperative signal or artifact, and option D mischaracterizes the defect because the finding is fluid-equivalent, not the intermediate signal of tendinosis.

confidence.statement: I can choose the correct imaging plane for each shoulder structure and follow a repeatable search pattern that evaluates every key structure without skipping any.
I can distinguish rotator cuff tendinosis from partial-thickness and full-thickness tears and judge reparability from retraction, fatty infiltration, and atrophy.
I can identify subscapularis tears and recognize the associated biceps pulley lesion and medial biceps subluxation on axial images.
I can differentiate a SLAP tear from a normal sublabral recess using lateral or posterior contrast extension, margin irregularity, and cleft orientation.
I can recognize and distinguish Bankart, ALPSA, Perthes, and HAGL lesions and identify when glenoid bone loss or an off-track Hill-Sachs warrants CT bone-loss quantification.
I can identify the MRI findings of adhesive capsulitis at the rotator interval and axillary pouch and avoid mistaking them for a cuff-driven cause of pain.
I can trace a paralabral cyst to its notch and report the resulting suprascapular nerve denervation edema or muscle atrophy beyond the labral tear itself.
I can distinguish a recurrent full-thickness cuff tear from expected postoperative tendon thickening, heterogeneous signal, and hardware artifact without over-calling failure.

searchPattern.name: Verify Protocol & Clinical Question
searchPattern.description: Confirm side, exam type, arthrogram status, available sequences, and the exact clinical question before narrowing your attention.
checklistItems: Confirm correct patient, side, and comparison imaging
Identify whether this is non-contrast MRI or MR arthrogram
Inventory planes and sequences: oblique coronal, oblique sagittal, axial, fluid-sensitive, T1
State the clinical question: cuff, instability, biceps/labrum, adhesive capsulitis, arthritis, trauma
Note image quality limitations and metal or motion artifact
pearls: Primary care sports medicine reads should start with the management question: rehab, injection, surgical referral, or urgent escalation.

searchPattern.name: Bones, Alignment & AC Joint
searchPattern.description: Assess humeral head position, glenoid bone stock, marrow edema, occult fracture, acromial morphology, and AC joint degeneration.
checklistItems: Check glenohumeral alignment and posterior/anterior subluxation
Look for fracture, marrow edema, Hill-Sachs or reverse Hill-Sachs lesion
Evaluate glenoid bone loss or bony Bankart fragment
Assess AC joint osteoarthritis and distal clavicle edema
Look for os acromiale or acromial stress reaction when impingement symptoms persist
pearls: Bone bruising often explains pain after instability even when the labral tear is subtle.
AC joint edema can be clinically dominant even when cuff findings are mild.

searchPattern.name: Rotator Cuff Tendons
searchPattern.description: Trace supraspinatus, infraspinatus, subscapularis, and teres minor from musculotendinous junction to insertion, then classify tendinosis or tear.
checklistItems: Supraspinatus: tendinosis, bursal-sided tear, articular-sided tear, full-thickness tear
Infraspinatus: posterior extension, delamination, full-thickness component
Subscapularis: upper-border tear, footprint involvement, lesser tuberosity attachment
Teres minor: edema or fatty change suggesting axillary nerve/quadrilateral space issue
For tears: report side, depth, AP size, retraction, and exposed footprint
pearls: Subscapularis tears are easy to undercall and matter because they destabilize the biceps pulley.
A full-thickness tear is a communication from articular to bursal surface, often with fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa.

searchPattern.name: Rotator Cuff Muscles
searchPattern.description: Evaluate muscle bulk, edema, and fatty infiltration because reparability and urgency often depend on the muscle more than the tendon edge.
checklistItems: Check supraspinatus and infraspinatus volume in the supraspinous/infraspinous fossae
Assess fatty infiltration qualitatively and compare muscle to surrounding fat
Look for muscle edema after acute tear, strain, denervation, or Parsonage-Turner pattern
Identify retraction to humeral head, glenoid, or medial to glenoid level
Look for superior humeral head migration suggesting chronic cuff insufficiency
pearls: Massive tear plus marked atrophy/fatty infiltration changes the referral conversation from repair toward salvage options.

searchPattern.name: Labrum & Instability Lesions
searchPattern.description: Evaluate the labrum by quadrant, connect abnormalities to instability direction, and look for associated osseous defects.
checklistItems: Superior labrum and biceps anchor: SLAP pattern versus normal recess
Anteroinferior labrum: Bankart, Perthes, ALPSA, GLAD, bony Bankart
Posterior labrum: reverse Bankart, Kim lesion, posterior paralabral cyst
Inferior capsule/IGHL: HAGL or capsular tear pattern
Match labral findings to Hill-Sachs, reverse Hill-Sachs, or glenoid bone loss
pearls: For primary care sports medicine, the question is not just 'is the labrum torn?' but whether the pattern explains instability and needs surgical consultation.

searchPattern.name: Biceps, Pulley & Rotator Interval
searchPattern.description: Assess the long head biceps tendon from anchor to groove, then evaluate the pulley and rotator interval structures that stabilize it.
checklistItems: Biceps anchor: fraying, SLAP extension, detachment
Intra-articular biceps: tendinosis, split tear, partial tear, rupture
Bicipital groove: tenosynovitis, subluxation, dislocation
Pulley: coracohumeral ligament, superior glenohumeral ligament, upper subscapularis
Rotator interval and capsule: thickening or edema suggesting adhesive capsulitis
pearls: Medial biceps subluxation should trigger a deliberate search for an upper-border subscapularis tear.

searchPattern.name: Cartilage, Capsule, Bursa & Synovium
searchPattern.description: Survey glenohumeral cartilage, capsule, recesses, subacromial-subdeltoid bursa, and synovium for pain generators and inflammatory patterns.
checklistItems: Glenohumeral cartilage: focal defect, diffuse thinning, osteophytes, subchondral cysts
Inferior capsule and axillary recess: thickening, edema, capsular volume loss
Subacromial-subdeltoid bursa: fluid, synovitis, communication with cuff tear
Loose bodies in axillary recess, biceps sheath, and subcoracoid recess
Synovitis or erosive pattern suggesting inflammatory arthropathy or infection
pearls: Adhesive capsulitis is a clinical diagnosis, but rotator interval edema and axillary capsular thickening can support the exam.

searchPattern.name: Nerves, Masses & Final Management Review
searchPattern.description: Finish with the spaces and soft tissues that can hide management-changing findings, then translate the MRI into a clear clinical next step.
checklistItems: Spinoglenoid notch and suprascapular notch: paralabral cyst or denervation changes
Quadrilateral space: mass, teres minor denervation, axillary nerve pattern
Deltoid, pectoralis major insertion, latissimus/teres major region if trauma history fits
Look for infection, tumor, or marrow replacement red flags
Summarize: rehab/injection candidate, surgical referral, urgent escalation, or imaging follow-up
pearls: A paralabral cyst with infraspinatus denervation is more than an incidental labral tear; it can change urgency and referral priority.

redFlags.finding: HAGL (humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament)
redFlags.action: The IGHL torn off the HUMERAL neck (J-sign axillary pouch) — the classic miss when attention is on the glenoid. A surgical instability lesion.
Seizure, electrocution, or fall on an adducted arm — easily missed; the head locks posteriorly. Read it on the axial.

redFlags.finding: Critical glenoid bone loss / off-track Hill-Sachs
redFlags.action: ≥20–25% glenoid loss or an engaging Hill-Sachs shifts a soft-tissue Bankart repair toward a bony procedure (Latarjet). CT quantifies best.

redFlags.finding: Irreparable cuff tear (high Goutallier + narrowed AHI)
redFlags.action: Goutallier ≥3 fatty infiltration with an acromiohumeral interval <7 mm signals a chronic/irreparable tear — changes the surgical conversation.
Hydroxyapatite is LOW signal on T1 AND T2 — easy to overlook on MRI; correlate the radiograph/ultrasound. It's an acute pain crisis, not a cuff-repair problem.

surgicalCorrelates.title: Supraspinatus footprint — MRI ↔ arthroscopy
surgicalCorrelates.body: Coronal MRI shows the cuff inserting on the greater-tuberosity footprint; a full-thickness tear is a fluid-filled gap. From the subacromial space at arthroscopy the same footprint is bared bone, and the tear pattern (crescent, U-, L-shaped) plus tendon mobility guide the repair construct.

surgicalCorrelates.title: SLAP — MRI ↔ arthroscopic probe
surgicalCorrelates.body: On MR-arthrography the superior labrum–biceps anchor is the SLAP zone, and a smooth sublabral recess can mimic a tear. Arthroscopy settles it — a probe tests whether the superior labrum is abnormally displaceable off the glenoid (vs a normal, smoothly contoured sublabral recess), and the dynamic peel-back sign (arm in ~90° abduction + maximal external rotation) shows the posterosuperior labrum peeling medially off the glenoid in a true type II SLAP.

pearls.text: Patte stage the retraction of a full-thickness supraspinatus tear on the coronal: stage 1 = stump near the footprint, stage 2 = at the humeral-head level, stage 3 = at the glenoid level (or more medial). Stage 3 retraction predicts poor reparability.

Goutallier/Fuchs fatty atrophy is the key reparability marker: grade 3-4 (fat equal to or exceeding muscle) on the sagittal Y-view predicts irreparability and re-tear regardless of tear size. Always comment on muscle quality, not just the tendon.

Distinguish tendinosis (intermediate T2 signal, no fluid-bright gap) from a partial tear (fluid-bright defect not spanning the full thickness) from a full-thickness tear (fluid-signal cleft from articular to bursal surface). Only the last communicates the joint with the subacromial bursa.

Grade articular-surface partial tears by depth: a defect greater than 50% of the ~12 mm footprint (a high-grade PASTA lesion, roughly >6 mm) generally crosses the surgical threshold for repair rather than debridement.

The supraspinatus 'critical zone,' about 1 cm proximal to the footprint, is hypovascular and the most common site of degenerative tears. Scrutinize it on every fluid-sensitive coronal and sagittal image.

On a fluid-sensitive sagittal at the level of the coracoid base (the 'Y' or Mercedes view), the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis are all profiled — the standard plane for grading Goutallier fatty atrophy and muscle bulk.

A torn subscapularis is easy to miss: look for tendon discontinuity at the lesser tuberosity on axial images, fatty atrophy on the sagittal Y-view, and medial subluxation of the long head of biceps as a secondary sign.

The biceps pulley (CHL + SGHL sling) stabilizes the long head of biceps at the groove entrance; a pulley lesion lets it sublux medially over or under the subscapularis. Assess it on axial images at the top of the bicipital groove, and if the biceps sits medial to the lesser tuberosity, interrogate the subscapularis insertion directly.

A normal sublabral recess is a smooth, medially-oriented cleft at the 11-1 o'clock position that follows the glenoid contour; a SLAP tear extends laterally/posteriorly, is irregular and wide (>2-3 mm), and tracks fluid or contrast into the labral substance.

The anteroinferior labroligamentous complex (3-6 o'clock) is the instability hot spot: a Bankart tear here in a young patient after anterior dislocation predicts recurrence. Trace the IGHL anterior band to its labral attachment.

Differentiate anteroinferior labral variants: Bankart = detached labrum with periosteal disruption; ALPSA = labroligamentous sleeve avulsed and displaced medially, healing malpositioned; Perthes = labrum detached but periosteum intact (may only show on ABER); GLAD = labral tear with an articular cartilage divot.

A HAGL lesion (humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament) produces a 'J-sign' axillary pouch on coronal images and is a commonly missed cause of instability — it is repaired on the humeral side, not at the labrum, so it must be reported.

Quantify glenoid bone loss before instability surgery: roughly >20-25% loss favors a bony augmentation (Latarjet) over isolated soft-tissue Bankart repair. CT with 3D 'en-face' glenoid reconstruction is the reference standard for measurement.

Apply the glenoid-track concept: the track measures ~83% of the glenoid diameter minus glenoid bone loss. A Hill-Sachs whose medial margin lies medial to the track is 'off-track,' engages, and mandates remplissage or a bone procedure rather than isolated Bankart repair.

A Hill-Sachs lesion is a posterolateral humeral head impaction fracture, typically at or above the level of the coracoid; the normal bare area lies more inferiorly. Measure its width and depth and pair it with the bony Bankart to determine on-track versus off-track status.

An empty bicipital groove on axial images means the long head of biceps has dislocated (usually medially, with a subscapularis tear) or fully ruptured and retracted. Follow the tendon distally toward the myotendinous junction to distinguish dislocation from rupture.

Subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid alone is nonspecific and accompanies bursitis or a cuff tear; call a full-thickness cuff tear only when a fluid-signal gap connects the joint to the bursa. Do not diagnose impingement off bursal fluid in isolation.

A laterally downsloping or hooked (Bigliani type III) acromion and subacromial spurs narrow the supraspinatus outlet and correlate with chronic impingement and cuff degeneration; report acromial morphology when there is bursal-surface fraying.

Os acromiale is an unfused acromial apophysis (most commonly meso-type) seen on axial images as a corticated ossicle; a mobile fragment can tilt into the outlet and cause impingement, and it must be flagged before acromioplasty.

Adhesive capsulitis is a soft-tissue MR diagnosis: thickening and edema of the axillary recess capsule (>4 mm), obliteration of the rotator-interval fat by enhancing soft tissue, and thickening of the coracohumeral ligament. The cuff itself is typically normal.

Calcific tendinosis shows blooming low-signal hydroxyapatite within the cuff (usually supraspinatus) on all sequences; surrounding edema and bursal fluid signal the painful resorptive phase. Correlate with radiographs or CT, as small calcifications are easily masked on MRI.

Quadrilateral space syndrome shows isolated fatty atrophy or denervation edema of the teres minor (± deltoid) from axillary nerve compression. Isolated teres minor changes should prompt a search for a paralabral cyst, fibrous band, or mass in the quadrilateral space.

Parsonage-Turner (neuralgic amyotrophy) presents as diffuse T2 denervation edema across muscles in a non-compressive, often multi-nerve distribution (commonly supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and deltoid) with no compressive mass — pain preceding weakness is the clinical tip-off.

A spinoglenoid-notch paralabral cyst compresses the suprascapular nerve distally, denervating the infraspinatus alone; a suprascapular-notch cyst affects both supraspinatus and infraspinatus. Always trace a posterior paralabral cyst to its parent posterosuperior labral tear.

Order direct MR arthrography for suspected labral pathology, SLAP tears, and subtle instability lesions; the distended joint and ABER positioning unmask non-displaced Perthes lesions and partial undersurface cuff tears that conventional MRI misses.

A sublabral foramen is a normal unattached anterosuperior labrum (1-3 o'clock) with the labrum intact below the equator; do not call it a Bankart. Mistaking this variant for a tear above the 3 o'clock position is a classic false positive.

The Buford complex is a congenital variant: absent anterosuperior labrum with a thick, cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament. Recognize it and do NOT surgically reattach the 'detached' labrum, which would restrict external rotation.

Anterosuperior labral variants (sublabral recess, sublabral foramen, Buford complex) are normal only above the glenoid equator (above 3 o'clock); any labral detachment at or below the equator (3-6 o'clock) is pathologic until proven otherwise.

Massive retracted cuff tears with a high-riding humerus (acromiohumeral interval <7 mm) and grade 3-4 fatty atrophy define cuff-tear arthropathy; these are generally irreparable and steer management toward reverse total shoulder arthroplasty rather than repair.

A reverse Hill-Sachs (anteromedial humeral head impaction) plus a reverse Bankart (posteroinferior labral tear) signals prior posterior dislocation — easily overlooked because the humeral head can look 'reduced.' Suspect it after seizures, electrocution, or a posteriorly directed fall.

items.label: Oblique coronal (parallel to supraspinatus)
items.detail: Workhorse for supraspinatus and infraspinatus tears, tendon footprint, retraction, marrow edema, and the AC joint. Trace the cuff from musculotendinous junction to the greater tuberosity.

items.label: Oblique sagittal (parallel to the glenoid face)
items.detail: Best for muscle bulk and fatty infiltration (Goutallier grading on the 'Y' view), localizing which tendon is torn, the rotator interval, and acromial morphology / os acromiale.

items.label: Axial
items.detail: Best for the subscapularis, the long head of biceps in/out of its groove (medial subluxation), the labrum (Bankart, reverse Bankart, ALPSA), glenoid bone loss, and Hill-Sachs / reverse Hill-Sachs.

items.label: Fluid-sensitive fat-saturated (T2 / PD FS)
items.detail: Shows edema, bursitis, fluid filling a cuff defect, and marrow injury. A tear is more convincing when fluid-bright signal reaches a tendon surface.

items.label: T1-weighted
items.detail: Anatomy, marrow replacement, muscle fatty infiltration, and arthrogram contrast (bright) when present.

items.label: Intermediate / PD (non-fat-sat)
items.detail: Helpful for tendon morphology and labral signal. Confirm a suspected finding on at least two planes.

items.label: Normal tendon
items.detail: Uniformly low signal, smooth, inserting on the footprint without surface-reaching fluid-bright defect. Tendinosis = thickening + intermediate signal WITHOUT a surface defect.

items.label: Magic-angle pitfall
items.detail: Intermediate signal in the distal supraspinatus where the tendon curves ~55° to B0 on short-TE (T1/PD) sequences. It resolves on T2 — do not call it a tear.

items.label: Normal labrum
items.detail: Triangular, uniformly low signal, firmly attached to the glenoid rim. Interpret superior labral signal in the context of age and sport before calling a SLAP tear.

items.label: Sublabral recess
items.detail: Normal smooth cleft under the superior labrum that parallels the glenoid and stops at the biceps anchor. A SLAP extends laterally into the labrum or posterior to the anchor with irregular margins.

items.label: Sublabral foramen & Buford complex
items.detail: Anterosuperior variants (1–3 o'clock). A Buford complex = absent anterosuperior labrum + cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament. Do not mistake either for a torn/detached labrum.

items.label: Long head of biceps
items.detail: Centered in the bicipital groove on axial images. Medial perching/subluxation should trigger a search for an upper-border subscapularis / pulley lesion.

items.label: Rotator interval & capsule
items.detail: Normal subcoracoid fat triangle and a thin coracohumeral ligament. Obliteration of that fat + CHL thickening + a thickened axillary pouch supports adhesive capsulitis.

measurementSections.title: Rotator cuff tear — what to report every time

items.label: Five-part description
items.detail: (1) Which tendon(s); (2) surface — articular-sided, bursal-sided, or full-thickness; (3) AP tear size; (4) retraction (Patte); (5) muscle atrophy / fatty infiltration (Goutallier). Surface + muscle quality are what change management.

items.label: Tendinosis vs partial vs full-thickness
items.detail: Tendinosis = thickening + intermediate signal, no surface defect. Partial = fluid-bright signal reaching ONE surface. Full-thickness = defect spanning articular-to-bursal with joint–bursa fluid communication.

items.label: Partial-tear depth
items.detail: Low-grade < 50% vs high-grade > 50% of tendon thickness. A high-grade partial tear behaves clinically closer to a significant structural lesion — not 'just tendinosis.'

items.label: Grade 0
items.detail: Normal muscle, no fat.

items.label: Grade 1
items.detail: Some fatty streaks within the muscle.

items.label: Grade 2
items.detail: Fatty infiltration present, but muscle > fat.

items.label: Grade 3
items.detail: Fat = muscle (50/50).

items.label: Grade 4
items.detail: Fat > muscle.

items.label: Why it matters
items.detail: Grade ≥ 3 predicts poor healing / higher retear and may indicate an irreparable tear. Assess on the oblique-sagittal 'Y' view at the scapular spine.

items.label: Stage 1
items.detail: Tendon stump near the footprint / greater tuberosity (minimal).

items.label: Stage 2
items.detail: Retraction to the level of the humeral head (moderate).

items.label: Stage 3
items.detail: Retraction to the glenoid level (advanced).

items.label: Why it matters
items.detail: Greater retraction + high Goutallier grade = harder repair and earlier surgical conversation.

items.label: Normal > 7 mm
items.detail: Distance from the humeral head to the undersurface of the acromion on the coronal image.

items.label: < 7 mm
items.detail: Suggests cuff insufficiency with superior humeral migration.

items.label: < 5 mm
items.detail: Severe — consistent with chronic massive cuff tear / cuff-tear arthropathy.

measurementSections.title: Instability — bone loss & the glenoid track

items.label: Critical glenoid bone loss
items.detail: Attritional or fracture-related glenoid bone loss approaching ~20–25% shifts management from isolated soft-tissue (Bankart) repair toward a bony procedure (e.g., Latarjet).

items.label: Glenoid track / engaging Hill-Sachs
items.detail: An 'off-track' Hill-Sachs engages the anterior glenoid rim and predicts recurrent instability — it changes the surgical plan even with limited glenoid loss.

items.label: Best test for bone loss
items.detail: CT (often 3D with the humeral head subtracted) quantifies glenoid and humeral bone loss most accurately. MRI/MRA may underestimate it.

items.label: Bankart
items.detail: Anteroinferior labrum detached with disrupted/torn periosteum (± bony Bankart = glenoid rim fracture).

items.label: Perthes
items.detail: Anteroinferior labrum stripped but the periosteum is INTACT and the labrum is minimally displaced — easily missed; ABER view helps.

items.label: ALPSA
items.detail: Labroligamentous complex displaced MEDIALLY along an intact periosteum and 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck — chronic/recurrent.

items.label: Hill-Sachs
items.detail: Posterolateral humeral head impaction from an anterior dislocation.

items.label: HAGL
items.detail: Humeral Avulsion of the Glenohumeral Ligament — IGHL torn from the HUMERAL neck with a 'J-sign' axillary pouch. A classic miss because attention is on the glenoid.

measurementSections.title: Protocol — MRI vs MR arthrogram vs CT

items.label: Non-contrast MRI (most primary care questions)
items.detail: Excellent for cuff tendinosis/tears, subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, AC joint, marrow/occult fracture, and adhesive-capsulitis support.

items.label: MR arthrogram
items.detail: Improves subtle labral/instability lesions, partial undersurface (articular-sided) cuff tears, and the postoperative labrum — when non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam.

items.label: CT (often 3D)
items.detail: Add when precise glenoid/humeral bone-loss quantification will change instability management.

items.label: MRI appearance
items.detail: A hydroxyapatite (calcific) deposit is LOW signal on T1 AND T2/fluid-sensitive sequences, so it is easy to overlook on MRI. Look for a focal low-signal focus in the tendon (often supraspinatus near the footprint) with surrounding edema. Radiographs and ultrasound show the deposit best — correlate.

items.label: Acute pain crisis
items.detail: Resorptive-phase calcific tendinitis causes severe atraumatic pain out of proportion to routine tendinopathy, often with florid peritendinous edema ± subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis (occasionally bursal rupture).

items.label: Management differs from tendinosis
items.detail: Usually conservative first (NSAIDs, activity modification, physical therapy); image-guided barbotage/aspiration or a subacromial injection is added for refractory or acute resorptive-phase pain. The point: it is not a structural cuff-repair problem — recognizing it changes the next step.

shoulder-mri-orientation.stem: A primary care sports medicine fellow is reviewing shoulder MRI for suspected instability and subtle labral pathology. Which protocol most often improves labral and capsuloligamentous evaluation compared with routine non-contrast MRI?

options.text: MR arthrography
T1-only non-contrast MRI
Ultrasound of the biceps groove only
Radiographs with internal rotation view only

shoulder-mri-orientation.explanation: MR arthrography distends the joint and can improve visualization of subtle labral, capsular, and undersurface cuff abnormalities. Routine MRI remains excellent for many cuff, bursa, marrow, arthritis, and adhesive capsulitis questions.

shoulder-mri-orientation.stem: Which shoulder MRI plane is most useful for assessing rotator cuff muscle bulk and fatty infiltration across the supraspinatus and infraspinatus fossae?

options.text: Axial
Oblique sagittal
Coronal localizer only
Scout images only

shoulder-mri-orientation.explanation: Oblique sagittal images are parallel to the glenoid face (i.e., perpendicular to the long axis of the supraspinatus tendon) and are the key plane for evaluating rotator cuff muscle bulk, atrophy, and fatty infiltration (Goutallier grading on the scapular-Y view).

shoulder-mri-orientation.stem: A fellow is reading a shoulder MRI for a possible full-thickness supraspinatus tear. The protocol includes T1-weighted, proton-density fat-suppressed (PD FS), and PD sequences. Which statement best describes how these sequences complement one another to confirm a surface-reaching fluid-filled defect and assess associated muscle/marrow changes?

options.text: PD FS best demonstrates the bright fluid signal tracking through a full-thickness defect into the subacromial bursa, while non-fat-suppressed PD/T1 best depict tendon and muscle anatomy and any fatty muscle atrophy.
PD FS alone is sufficient; the non-fat-suppressed sequences add no diagnostic value and only waste acquisition time.
T1 alone suffices, because joint and bursal fluid appears bright on T1 and is therefore the most conspicuous sign of a tear.
Gradient-echo sequences alone are preferred, because their magnetic-susceptibility effects maximize conspicuity of cuff tears and fluid.

shoulder-mri-orientation.explanation: On fluid-sensitive PD FS, fluid is bright and tracks through a full-thickness cuff defect into the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa, confirming a surface-reaching tear. Non-fat-suppressed PD and T1 give higher-SNR anatomic detail and show fatty muscle atrophy (Goutallier). Fluid is not bright on T1, and GRE blooming degrades cuff/fluid assessment.

shoulder-mri-orientation.stem: A 58-year-old primary care sports medicine patient presents with 3 weeks of severe shoulder pain and global passive range-of-motion loss after a minor rotator cuff strain. You obtain a shoulder MRI and want to assess whether adhesive capsulitis findings support the clinical presentation. Which sequences and imaging findings are most useful for this question?

options.text: T1-weighted images alone, because they show anatomy best and are sufficient to rule out a tear.

Axial and coronal fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed sequences to visualize rotator interval edema, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and axillary pouch capsular thickening—findings that support adhesive capsulitis in the appropriate clinical setting and help guide injection or therapy planning.
Gradient-echo sequences are essential because adhesive capsulitis produces blooming artifact that proves the diagnosis.
Sagittal images are the only plane needed; axial and coronal images are redundant for assessing capsular inflammation.

shoulder-mri-orientation.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis imaging findings—rotator interval edema, CHL thickening, axillary recess capsular thickening—are best seen on fluid-sensitive sequences and axial/coronal planes. MRI supports but does not replace the clinical diagnosis of global ROM loss; these imaging findings are management-relevant when they match the exam and guide injection-planning or exclusion of competing pathology.

shoulder-search-pattern.stem: After finding mild supraspinatus tendinosis, the reader stops and misses medial biceps subluxation from an upper-border subscapularis tear. What cognitive error does the search pattern help prevent?

options.text: Satisfaction of search
Recency bias
Lead-time bias
Verification bias

shoulder-search-pattern.explanation: Satisfaction of search occurs when finding one abnormality reduces vigilance for additional findings. The shoulder search pattern forces a complete review even after an obvious pain generator is found.

shoulder-search-pattern.stem: Which impression is most useful for a primary care sports medicine clinician?

options.text: Abnormal shoulder MRI.
Signal abnormality in the cuff and labrum.
Acute full-thickness supraspinatus tear with mild retraction and no advanced fatty atrophy; timely surgical referral is appropriate.
Degenerative changes are present.

shoulder-search-pattern.explanation: Management-relevant impressions name the dominant diagnosis, severity, chronicity/reparability clues, and clinical implication. Vague signal descriptions do not help the next-step decision.

shoulder-search-pattern.stem: You are implementing a systematic shoulder MRI search pattern as a primary care sports medicine fellow. Which step sequence is most logical for ensuring you capture management-changing pathology while avoiding satisfaction-of-search errors?

options.text: Start with the clinically suspected pathology, then skip any regions that appear normal to save time and move to the next case quickly.
Read the supraspinatus alone first; if it is normal, conclude the rotator cuff is intact and move to the labrum without checking the subscapularis, infraspinatus, or muscles.

Begin with protocol and clinical context, then evaluate bones and alignment, followed by rotator cuff tendons and muscles, labrum and instability lesions, biceps/pulley/rotator interval, capsule and bursa, and finally nerves and red flags—in that fixed order every time.
Use a different search order for each patient based on the clinical question, adapting the pattern to what you think is most likely to be abnormal.

shoulder-search-pattern.explanation: A fixed eight-step checklist is not a crutch—it is how experts protect themselves from predictable human misses like medial biceps subluxation, posterior labral tears, and denervation. Finishing the full pattern on every case is what keeps satisfactory findings (mild tendinosis) from hiding management-changing lesions (subscapularis tear with pulley involvement).

shoulder-search-pattern.stem: A fellow dictates an impression: 'Slight supraspinatus signal change; consider tendinosis versus small tear.' This phrasing has several problems for a primary care clinician. Which revised impression is most useful?

options.text: Signal abnormality present in the supraspinatus; clinical correlation needed.
Possible rotator cuff pathology; recommendation: advance imaging if symptoms persist.
Mild degenerative signal change in the cuff; significance unknown from imaging alone.
Low-grade supraspinatus tendinosis without discrete surface-reaching tear or retraction; no acute traumatic features; rehab-first strategy remains reasonable, though patient correlation advised.

shoulder-search-pattern.explanation: Structured impressions answer four questions: (1) What is the dominant diagnosis? (2) How severe? (3) What associated findings matter? (4) What is the management implication? Vague phrases like 'signal change' and 'consider' place the clinical burden back on the referring provider. Instead, name the severity, state whether a surface is involved, and guide the next visit (rehab-first vs. injection vs. referral).

shoulder-search-pattern.stem: On an MR arthrogram, you identify a smooth band of contrast undercutting the superior labrum at the biceps-labral junction, following the glenoid contour and stopping precisely at the biceps anchor. The margins are sharp and regular. What is the correct interpretation?

options.text: Normal sublabral recess—a common variant that should not be reported as a tear and does not require surgical follow-up.
SLAP tear with posterior extension; recommend arthroscopy for confirmation.
Sublabral foramen with partial labral detachment; borderline for referral.
Type II SLAP with biceps anchor instability; surgical consultation advised.

shoulder-search-pattern.explanation: A sublabral recess is a normal variant with a smooth, medial cleft that parallels the glenoid contour and stops at the biceps anchor. A true SLAP tear, by contrast, extends LATERALLY into the labral substance or POSTERIOR to the anchor with irregular, jagged margins. Mistaking a recess for a tear is a classic overcall that exposes an asymptomatic patient to unnecessary imaging and referral; two-plane confirmation and arthrographic morphology are key to the differentiation.

shoulder-rotator-cuff.stem: A supraspinatus tendon is thickened with intermediate signal but has no fluid-filled surface defect or fiber discontinuity. What is the best description?

options.text: Full-thickness tear
Tendinosis
Complete tendon rupture with retraction
Bankart lesion

shoulder-rotator-cuff.explanation: Tendinosis produces tendon thickening and intermediate signal without a discrete articular-sided, bursal-sided, or full-thickness defect.

shoulder-rotator-cuff.stem: Which additional information is most important when reporting a full-thickness rotator cuff tear?

options.text: Only whether bursal fluid is present
Only the patient age
Tendons involved, tear size, retraction, muscle atrophy, and fatty infiltration
The number of localizer images

shoulder-rotator-cuff.explanation: Tendons involved, AP size, retraction, and muscle quality are management-changing details that influence referral urgency and repairability.

shoulder-rotator-cuff.stem: A partial-thickness supraspinatus tear shows fluid-bright signal reaching only the deep surface that faces the glenohumeral joint. How is this best classified?

options.text: Bursal-sided partial tear
Articular-sided (PASTA) partial tear
Full-thickness tear
Tendinosis

shoulder-rotator-cuff.explanation: The articular (deep) surface faces the joint; an articular-sided partial tear (PASTA) is the most common partial tear. Bursal-sided tears involve the superficial surface, and a full-thickness tear reaches both surfaces.

shoulder-rotator-cuff.stem: On axial images the long head of biceps is displaced medially out of the bicipital groove. Which tendon should you deliberately scrutinize?

options.text: Infraspinatus
Teres minor
Upper-border subscapularis
Supraspinatus only

shoulder-rotator-cuff.explanation: Medial biceps displacement is a red flag for a biceps pulley / upper-border subscapularis lesion — the classic "hidden lesion." Inspect the subscapularis on axial and sagittal images.

shoulder-rotator-cuff.stem: A 58-year-old with a full-thickness supraspinatus tear is referred for surgical evaluation. On oblique-sagittal MRI at the scapular Y-view, the supraspinatus muscle shows intramuscular fat streaks that are approximately equal to the residual muscle volume. Using Goutallier grading, what does this finding indicate, and how should it influence surgical decision-making?

options.text: Goutallier grade 1 (minimal streaking); this is a favorable prognostic sign that predicts excellent repair outcomes regardless of tear size.

Goutallier grade 3 (fat ≥ muscle); this predicts poor healing and may indicate an irreparable tear or mandate low-expectation repair counseling because advanced fatty infiltration significantly reduces repairability and functional outcome.
Goutallier grade 2 (fat < muscle); this is irrelevant to repair potential and does not change the surgical recommendation beyond standard repair.
Goutallier grade 4 (complete fatty replacement); although severe, grade 4 is still suitable for routine rotator cuff repair with the same success rates as lower grades.

shoulder-rotator-cuff.explanation: Goutallier grades reflect muscle quality and predict repairability: grades 0–2 generally favor repair; grades 3–4 indicate potential irreparability or poor functional outcome even if repair is technically possible. Assessment on the oblique-sagittal Y-view is essential for accurate grading and informs preoperative counseling.

shoulder-labrum-instability.stem: A first-time anterior shoulder dislocation MRI shows an anteroinferior labral detachment and a posterolateral humeral head impaction injury. What is the most coherent diagnosis?

options.text: Adhesive capsulitis
Bankart lesion with Hill-Sachs injury
Isolated AC joint arthritis
Quadrilateral space syndrome

shoulder-labrum-instability.explanation: Anterior instability commonly produces an anteroinferior labral Bankart lesion and a posterolateral humeral head Hill-Sachs impaction injury.

shoulder-labrum-instability.stem: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is identified. What associated finding should be actively checked?

options.text: Infraspinatus denervation edema or atrophy
Patellar tendon rupture
Medial meniscal extrusion
Achilles tendinosis

shoulder-labrum-instability.explanation: A spinoglenoid notch cyst can compress the suprascapular nerve branch to infraspinatus, causing denervation edema acutely or fatty atrophy chronically.

shoulder-labrum-instability.stem: On an MR arthrogram of the superior labrum, which feature favors a true SLAP tear over a normal sublabral recess?

options.text: A smooth medial cleft that parallels the glenoid and stops at the biceps anchor
Contrast extending laterally into the labral substance or posterior to the biceps anchor with irregular margins
A cleft located at the 12 o’clock position
A cleft that measures less than 2 mm

shoulder-labrum-instability.explanation: A sublabral recess is smooth, medial, parallels the glenoid, and stops at the biceps anchor. Lateral extension into the labrum or posterior to the anchor with irregular margins favors a SLAP tear. A 12 o’clock location is common to both and does not discriminate.

shoulder-labrum-instability.stem: In recurrent anterior instability, which finding most increases the importance of surgical referral and may warrant CT for precise quantification?

options.text: A small soft-tissue Bankart with no osseous injury
Significant glenoid bone loss or an engaging / off-track Hill-Sachs lesion
Mild subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis
A normal sublabral recess

shoulder-labrum-instability.explanation: Significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20%) or an engaging / off-track Hill-Sachs shifts management toward a bony procedure (e.g., Latarjet) and is best quantified by CT, often 3D. An isolated soft-tissue Bankart does not carry the same implication.

shoulder-labrum-instability.stem: A 28-year-old overhead athlete with posterior shoulder pain undergoes shoulder MRI with non-contrast and MR arthrography. Axial images show a hypoattenuating cleft at the posterior labrum with irregular margins and edema in the adjacent soft tissue. The referring clinician's note only states "labral tear." Which additional information is essential to guide the next diagnostic and management step?

options.text: Report only the tear size in millimeters, because that is the only measurement that influences surgical decision-making.
Describe the signal intensity of the tear relative to fluid, since signal alone is sufficient to communicate the clinical urgency.

Specify the quadrant location (e.g., posterior or 6-9 o'clock position) and pattern (tear vs. degenerative fraying vs. reverse Bankart), as posterior labral injuries require different imaging follow-up and surgical considerations than anterior or superior patterns.
Simply confirm that a labral abnormality is present and defer all further characterization to the orthopedic surgeon, as MRI interpretation should not include quadrant or pattern details.

shoulder-labrum-instability.explanation: Labral pathology must be localized by quadrant and classified by pattern (traumatic tear, degenerative fraying, instability variant, or associated with cyst/denervation) because the clinical significance, likelihood of symptoms, and surgical approach differ between anterior (Bankart), superior (SLAP), and posterior labral injuries. 'Labral tear' alone does not guide management.

shoulder-biceps-interval-ac.stem: Medial subluxation of the long head biceps tendon from the groove should trigger a deliberate search for which associated lesion?

options.text: Upper-border subscapularis tear or biceps pulley lesion
Posterior horn medial meniscus tear
Distal radius fracture
Achilles rupture

shoulder-biceps-interval-ac.explanation: The biceps pulley and upper subscapularis stabilize the long head biceps. Medial biceps subluxation is a red flag for pulley/subscapularis pathology.

shoulder-biceps-interval-ac.stem: MRI shows rotator interval edema, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and axillary capsular thickening in a patient with global passive range-of-motion loss. What clinical diagnosis do these findings support?

options.text: Adhesive capsulitis
Anterior shoulder dislocation
Massive acute pectoralis tear
Normal variant with no clinical relevance

shoulder-biceps-interval-ac.explanation: Rotator interval edema, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and axillary capsular thickening support adhesive capsulitis when the clinical exam shows global passive range-of-motion loss.

shoulder-biceps-interval-ac.stem: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain and clicking in the bicipital groove. MRI shows signal abnormality and mild thickening of the long head biceps tendon in the intra-articular segment with surrounding synovial fluid. The biceps anchor is intact and the tendon remains centered in the groove. No rotator cuff tear is identified. Which term best describes this finding, and what management approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Biceps rupture; this requires urgent surgical referral and tenodesis to restore elbow flexion and supination strength.
SLAP tear; this requires MR arthrography confirmation and typically surgical referral in active patients, as an anchor tear would show contrast undercutting the labral attachment.
Biceps pulley failure; this would present with medial biceps subluxation or dislocation out of the groove, which is not seen here with the tendon centered.

Biceps tenosynovitis; this is a synovial inflammatory response around the tendon that is typically managed conservatively with NSAIDs, activity modification, and physical therapy focused on scapular stability and rotator cuff strengthening.

shoulder-biceps-interval-ac.explanation: Biceps tenosynovitis manifests as fluid surrounding an intact, centered tendon without surface-reaching defects or rupture. The distinction between tendinosis (tendon thickening and signal change) versus tenosynovitis (sheath fluid) versus tear is critical—tenosynovitis and low-grade tendinosis are often rehab-first findings, whereas partial or complete rupture and pulley failure warrant referral consideration.

shoulder-biceps-interval-ac.stem: A 58-year-old with superior shoulder pain localized to the AC joint on cross-body adduction testing undergoes shoulder MRI. Coronal and axial images show joint-space narrowing, hypertrophic osteophytes, and marrow edema in both the distal clavicle and the lateral acromial process. There is no AC joint capsular hypertrophy, and the adjacent supraspinatus tendon is intact. How should you interpret these findings in your report?

options.text: AC joint osteoarthritis with acute-on-chronic change; the marrow edema in both articulating surfaces indicates recent stress or microtrauma. This finding is clinically relevant and should be matched to the patient's focal AC tenderness and cross-body adduction pain before concluding it is the pain generator, and inferior osteophytes should be assessed for contact with the cuff or bursa.

Incidental AC joint changes of no clinical significance; AC osteophytes are common incidental findings, but the presence of marrow edema indicates acute inflammation that may be symptomatic and warrant correlation with clinical exam.

AC joint septic arthritis requiring urgent intervention; while aggressive edema and effusion can suggest infection, uncomplicated osteoarthritis with reactive marrow edema is far more common in this age group without systemic signs.

Supraspinatus impingement from AC osteophytes requiring surgical distal clavicle resection; inferior AC osteophytes can contact the cuff, but this finding alone does not mandate surgery—correlation with symptoms, exam, and conservative treatment response guides referral.

shoulder-biceps-interval-ac.explanation: AC joint osteoarthritis is common, but the clinically relevant finding is often marrow edema or capsular hypertrophy, not the mere presence of osteophytes. Match MRI findings to focal AC tenderness and cross-body adduction pain; imaging alone should not drive surgery. Always assess whether inferior osteophytes contact the rotator cuff or bursa, as this influences the surgical discussion. The distinction between asymptomatic degenerative changes and clinically important AC pathology rests on imaging sign (edema, capsular thickening) plus clinical correlation.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.stem: A massive chronic supraspinatus/infraspinatus tear has retraction to the glenoid, advanced fatty infiltration, and superior humeral migration. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: Low-grade tendinosis suitable for routine reassurance only
Cuff tear arthropathy pattern with limited primary repairability
Normal age-related finding that should not be reported
Isolated SLAP tear

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.explanation: Massive chronic cuff tear with advanced fatty atrophy and superior migration suggests cuff tear arthropathy and changes referral expectations away from simple primary repair.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.stem: A paralabral cyst with infraspinatus denervation edema is seen on shoulder MRI. Why is this management-relevant?

options.text: It may indicate suprascapular nerve compression and should not be dismissed as an incidental small labral tear.
It proves the ACL is torn.
It means all rotator cuff tendons are normal.
It is always a contraindication to physical therapy.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.explanation: Paralabral cysts can compress the suprascapular nerve, producing denervation changes. This is a referral-relevant finding even when the labral tear itself appears small.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.stem: A 52-year-old with chronic shoulder pain undergoes MRI. Oblique coronal images show a fluid-distended subacromial-subdeltoid bursa with multiple small intra-bursal filling defects. The supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons are intact without full-thickness tear. Which additional finding should prompt you to reconsider the diagnosis beyond simple mechanical bursitis?

options.text: Mild AC joint osteophytes contacting the bursa, which explains reactive bursitis from subacromial impingement.
Synovitis with rice bodies on the bursal fluid-sensitive images, raising suspicion for an inflammatory or rheumatologic process rather than a structural cuff tear or impingement.
Low-signal calcific deposit within the supraspinatus tendon, indicating acute calcific tendinitis with secondary reactive bursitis.
Small bursal fluid collection measuring <2 mL, which is a normal variant not requiring clinical follow-up.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.explanation: Bursitis is a finding, not always a diagnosis. Always pair bursal fluid assessment with cuff integrity, synovial character (rice bodies, enhancement), and marrow/capsular changes to direct the differential toward mechanical, inflammatory, or infectious etiologies.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.stem: A 68-year-old male presents with acute shoulder pain after a minor fall. MRI shows diffuse humeral head marrow edema on PD FS images with a subtle subchondral low-signal band on T1 at the weight-bearing surface of the humeral head. The overlying articular cartilage appears intact. There is no history of prior shoulder trauma or steroid use. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Acute traumatic full-thickness rotator cuff tear with superior humeral migration, which would show a tendon defect with retraction.
Avascular necrosis of the humeral head, which typically shows a larger geographic zone of necrosis and is associated with corticosteroid use or sickle cell disease.
Subchondral insufficiency fracture (analogous to SIFK in the knee), occurring in osteoporotic bone under normal loading without a known prior major trauma.
Simple bone contusion from the fall, which would show diffuse marrow edema without a discrete low-T1 subchondral line.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.explanation: A discrete low-T1 subchondral line (best seen on T1) with surrounding marrow edema in the appropriate clinical context (older patient, acute onset, no major trauma) should raise suspicion for insufficiency fracture. This changes weight-bearing and surgical considerations and may not fit the typical sports-medicine pain pattern.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.stem: You are formulating the MRI impression for a primary care sports medicine clinician. Which statement best reflects how to structure a high-yield shoulder MRI impression for maximum clinical utility?

options.text: List all anatomic findings in order of location (AC joint, bursa, labrum, cuff) regardless of clinical relevance, to ensure comprehensive documentation.
Focus exclusively on the most obvious finding (e.g., 'mild AC joint arthritis') and defer mention of coexisting pathology to avoid cluttering the impression.
Avoid any reference to severity or urgency unless the finding is clearly life-threatening, so as not to over-triage routine findings.

Lead with the finding most likely to change management (e.g., 'Spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst with infraspinatus denervation edema') and include severity language, associated findings, and the next-step hierarchy so the clinician can prioritize referral and treatment decisions.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.explanation: A useful shoulder MRI impression answers: (1) What is the dominant diagnosis? (2) How severe is it? (3) What associated findings matter? (4) What should not be missed? Lead with the finding that changes management, not the most obvious one—the second finding often matters more than the first.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.stem: A 28-year-old competitive powerlifter feels a sudden 'pop' at the bottom of a heavy bench press, with the arm extended and abducted. He develops medial chest bruising and a soft, dropped-out anterior axillary fold. MRI shows a hematoma with a medially retracted tendon and no tissue at the lateral lip of the intertubercular groove. Which finding most directly indicates the need for surgical referral?

options.text: Feathery perifascial edema within the pectoralis major belly with intact architecture
Complete avulsion of the tendon from its humeral insertion with an empty bicipital groove
Fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa communicating with the joint
Intermediate signal and thickening of the pectoralis tendon without a discrete gap

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.explanation: The mechanism (eccentric load, arm extended/abducted at the bottom of a bench press), medial ecchymosis, and axillary-fold defect are classic for pectoralis major rupture, and the empty bicipital groove with a retracted tendon localizes a complete avulsion at the humeral insertion — the surgically relevant lesion that warrants repair in an active patient. Feathery intramuscular edema (A) is a grade 1 strain managed with rehab, and intermediate tendon signal without a gap (D) is tendinosis, not an avulsion; bursal fluid (C) is a cuff/bursa finding unrelated to the pectoralis.

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.stem: A 24-year-old wakeboarder is jerked forward when the tow rope catches with the arm flexed and abducted, and reports posterior axillary pain. MRI shows feathery edema PLUS a discrete fluid cleft with partial fiber disruption at the myotendinous junction of the latissimus dorsi/teres major, with most fibers intact and no retraction. How is this best characterized and managed?

options.text: Grade 1 overstretch injury with no fiber disruption, managed with rest
Grade 2 partial myotendinous strain, generally managed conservatively
Deltoid denervation from axillary nerve injury
Pectoralis major humeral avulsion with an empty bicipital groove

shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss.explanation: Partial fiber disruption with a discrete fluid cleft but preserved continuity and no retraction is a grade 2 partial strain, and the myotendinous junction is the expected site; latissimus dorsi/teres major strains in water-skiers/wakeboarders and throwers are typically treated conservatively. A grade 1 injury (A) shows only perifascial feathery edema with intact architecture and no fiber disruption or fluid cleft, deltoid denervation (C) produces diffuse muscle edema in a nerve territory rather than an MTJ cleft, and a pectoralis avulsion (D) involves a different muscle and the bicipital groove.

cards.question: When is an MR arthrogram preferred over a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI?
cards.answer: When subtle labral/instability lesions, an articular-sided (undersurface) partial cuff tear, or the postoperative labrum are in question and non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam. Intra-articular contrast distends the joint and outlines the labrum.

cards.question: What does a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI answer well?
cards.answer: Most primary care questions: cuff tendinosis/tears, subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, AC joint disease, marrow/occult fracture, and findings supporting adhesive capsulitis.

cards.question: Which plane is the workhorse for supraspinatus/infraspinatus tears and the AC joint?
cards.answer: Oblique coronal (parallel to the supraspinatus). Trace the cuff from the musculotendinous junction to the greater tuberosity footprint.

cards.question: Which plane best shows the subscapularis, the biceps in/out of its groove, the labrum (Bankart), and Hill-Sachs?
cards.answer: Axial. The oblique sagittal is best for muscle bulk/fatty infiltration (Goutallier 'Y' view), which tendon is torn, the rotator interval, and acromial morphology.

cards.question: Which sequence makes a cuff tear most convincing, and why?
cards.answer: Fluid-sensitive fat-saturated (T2/PD FS): a tear is convincing when fluid-bright signal reaches a tendon surface or fills a defect.

cards.question: What is the magic-angle pitfall in the shoulder?
cards.answer: Intermediate signal in the distal supraspinatus where it curves ~55° to the main field on short-TE (T1/PD) sequences. It resolves on T2 — do not call it a tear.

cards.question: For primary care sports medicine, what is the goal when reading a shoulder MRI?
cards.answer: Answer the management question — is there a structural/surgical lesion, a red flag, or a finding that changes the next step (referral, injection, rehab)? — rather than cataloguing every degenerative change.

cards.question: Which high-miss areas is a shoulder search pattern designed to catch?
cards.answer: Upper-border subscapularis, the biceps pulley / medial biceps subluxation, the posterior and posterosuperior labrum, suprascapular/spinoglenoid denervation, and the AC joint.

cards.question: What is satisfaction-of-search, and how do you guard against it on shoulder MRI?
cards.answer: Stopping after the first obvious finding (e.g., a supraspinatus tear) and missing a second lesion. Finish the full checklist every time — a cuff tear does not exclude a labral tear, a biceps lesion, or denervation.

cards.question: Name three superior-labral 'don't-overcall' variants.
cards.answer: Sublabral recess, sublabral foramen, and the Buford complex (absent anterosuperior labrum + a cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament).

cards.question: How do you distinguish a sublabral recess from a SLAP tear?
cards.answer: A recess is smooth, medial, parallels the glenoid, and stops at the biceps anchor. A SLAP extends laterally into the labrum or posterior to the anchor with irregular margins.

cards.question: How does tendinosis look versus a cuff tear?
cards.answer: Tendinosis = tendon thickening + intermediate signal WITHOUT a surface-reaching fluid-bright defect. A tear shows fluid-bright signal reaching a surface (partial) or spanning articular-to-bursal (full-thickness).

cards.question: Articular- versus bursal-sided partial tear — which surface is which, and which is more common?
cards.answer: Articular = the deep surface facing the joint (the most common partial tear, 'PASTA'). Bursal = the superficial surface under the subacromial bursa.

cards.question: How is partial-tear depth graded, and why does it matter?
cards.answer: Low-grade <50% vs high-grade >50% of tendon thickness. A high-grade partial behaves clinically closer to a significant structural lesion — not 'just tendinosis.'

cards.question: What five things should every full-thickness cuff tear report include?
cards.answer: (1) which tendon(s); (2) surface/thickness; (3) AP tear size; (4) retraction (Patte stage); (5) muscle atrophy / fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade). Surface and muscle quality are what change management.

cards.question: What does the Patte stage describe?
cards.answer: Tendon retraction: Stage 1 near the footprint, Stage 2 to the humeral head, Stage 3 to the glenoid. More retraction = harder repair.

cards.question: What Goutallier grade predicts poor healing / an irreparable tear, and where is it assessed?
cards.answer: Grade ≥3 (fat ≥ muscle). Assess on the oblique-sagittal 'Y' view at the scapular spine.

cards.question: Medial subluxation of the biceps out of its groove should trigger a search for what?
cards.answer: An upper-border subscapularis tear / biceps pulley lesion — the classic 'hidden lesion.' Inspect the subscapularis on axial and sagittal images.

cards.question: What does a normal labrum look like?
cards.answer: Triangular, uniformly low signal, firmly attached to the glenoid rim. Interpret superior labral signal in the context of age and sport before calling a SLAP tear.

cards.question: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is found — what associated finding must you check?
cards.answer: Infraspinatus denervation edema (acute) or fatty atrophy (chronic) from compression of the suprascapular nerve branch.

cards.question: What is the classic bipolar lesion of an anterior shoulder dislocation?
cards.answer: An anteroinferior labral Bankart lesion plus a posterolateral humeral-head Hill-Sachs impaction.

cards.question: Define Bankart, Perthes, ALPSA, and HAGL.
cards.answer: Bankart (soft-tissue) = anteroinferior labrum detached with torn periosteum; the osseous Bankart variant is an anteroinferior glenoid rim fracture (it drives the bone-loss/surgical-referral assessment). Perthes = labrum stripped but periosteum intact and minimally displaced (easily missed). ALPSA = labroligamentous complex displaced medially and 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck (chronic). HAGL = inferior glenohumeral ligament avulsed from the HUMERAL side ('J-sign' axillary pouch).

cards.question: Which instability findings push toward surgery and warrant CT quantification?
cards.answer: Significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20%, with lower subcritical thresholds increasingly used) or an engaging / off-track Hill-Sachs. CT (often 3D) quantifies bone loss best — MRI/MRA may underestimate it.

cards.question: Where do you check the long head of biceps, and what is normal versus abnormal?
cards.answer: Centered in the bicipital groove on axial images. Fluid tracking only around the tendon reflects the joint's fluid; medial perching/subluxation out of the groove is abnormal and a red flag.

cards.question: Medial biceps subluxation implies injury to what, and which tendon should you scrutinize?
cards.answer: The biceps pulley (coracohumeral / superior glenohumeral ligament sling). Scrutinize the upper-border subscapularis — the 'hidden lesion.'

cards.question: What MRI findings support adhesive capsulitis?
cards.answer: Obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle in the rotator interval, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and a thickened/edematous axillary pouch and capsule. Correlate with painful global ROM loss — the diagnosis is clinical.

cards.question: When does AC joint arthrosis matter on MRI?
cards.answer: When it correlates with focal AC tenderness: look for capsular hypertrophy, marrow edema on BOTH sides of the joint, and inferior osteophytes contacting the cuff. Asymptomatic AC degeneration is common — don't over-attribute pain to it.

cards.question: What acromiohumeral interval values signal cuff insufficiency and cuff-tear arthropathy?
cards.answer: Normal >7 mm. <7 mm suggests cuff insufficiency with superior humeral migration. <5 mm is consistent with a chronic massive tear / cuff-tear arthropathy.

cards.question: What is the imaging clue and the management pivot for calcific tendinitis?
cards.answer: A focal LOW-signal deposit on T1 AND T2 (easy to miss) with surrounding edema, often in the supraspinatus near the footprint. It is usually managed conservatively first (NSAIDs, activity modification, physical therapy); ultrasound-guided barbotage/aspiration or a subacromial injection is added for refractory or acutely painful (resorptive-phase) cases. The key point: it is NOT a structural cuff-repair problem. Radiographs/ultrasound show the deposit best.

cards.question: Match the denervated muscle to the paralabral cyst location.
cards.answer: Spinoglenoid notch cyst → infraspinatus denervation. Suprascapular notch cyst → BOTH supraspinatus and infraspinatus.

cards.question: Name shoulder MRI red flags that change management urgently.
cards.answer: Suspected septic arthritis/osteomyelitis, an aggressive marrow-replacing or soft-tissue mass, and acute occult fracture. Communicate directly and don't anchor on degenerative findings.

cards.question: What makes a shoulder MRI impression 'management-changing'?
cards.answer: It states the structural diagnosis, its severity/repairability (tendon, size, retraction, muscle quality; bone loss; denervation), and the implied next step (rehab, injection, referral) — rather than a list of every degenerative change.

cards.question: What are the three MRI grades of a muscle strain, and where do strains most commonly occur?
cards.answer: Grade 1 = perifascial 'feathery' edema with intact architecture; Grade 2 = partial tear with fiber disruption plus fluid/hematoma; Grade 3 = complete tear with retraction. The myotendinous junction is the most common site.

cards.question: What is the classic mechanism and the most surgically relevant tear location for a pectoralis major rupture?
cards.answer: Eccentric load with the arm extended/abducted — classically the bottom of a bench press. The most surgically relevant location is avulsion of the tendon from its humeral insertion at the lateral lip of the bicipital (intertubercular) groove; the sternocostal head tears most often (inferior sternocostal fibers first).

cards.question: What MRI sign indicates a complete humeral-sided avulsion of the pectoralis major, and what should the report state?
cards.answer: The 'empty bicipital groove' — the avulsed tendon is absent from the lateral lip of the intertubercular groove (partial/MTJ tears retain tendon there). Report which head(s), tear location (intramuscular vs MTJ vs humeral avulsion), completeness, and retraction; acute complete avulsions in active patients are referred for surgical repair.

shoulder-m1-q1.stem: A primary care sports medicine fellow is reviewing shoulder MRI for suspected instability and subtle labral pathology. Which protocol most often improves labral and capsuloligamentous evaluation compared with routine non-contrast MRI?

options.text: MR arthrography
T1-only non-contrast MRI
Ultrasound of the biceps groove only
Radiographs with internal rotation view only

shoulder-m1-q1.explanation: MR arthrography distends the joint and can improve visualization of subtle labral, capsular, and undersurface cuff abnormalities. Routine MRI remains excellent for many cuff, bursa, marrow, arthritis, and adhesive capsulitis questions.

shoulder-m1-q2.stem: Which shoulder MRI plane is most useful for assessing rotator cuff muscle bulk and fatty infiltration across the supraspinatus and infraspinatus fossae?

options.text: Axial
Oblique sagittal
Coronal localizer only
Scout images only

shoulder-m1-q2.explanation: Oblique sagittal images are parallel to the glenoid face (i.e., perpendicular to the long axis of the supraspinatus tendon) and are the key plane for evaluating rotator cuff muscle bulk, atrophy, and fatty infiltration (Goutallier grading on the scapular-Y view).

shoulder-m1-q3.stem: A fellow is reading a shoulder MRI for a possible full-thickness supraspinatus tear. The protocol includes T1-weighted, proton-density fat-suppressed (PD FS), and PD sequences. Which statement best describes how these sequences complement one another to confirm a surface-reaching fluid-filled defect and assess associated muscle/marrow changes?

options.text: PD FS best demonstrates the bright fluid signal tracking through a full-thickness defect into the subacromial bursa, while non-fat-suppressed PD/T1 best depict tendon and muscle anatomy and any fatty muscle atrophy.
PD FS alone is sufficient; the non-fat-suppressed sequences add no diagnostic value and only waste acquisition time.
T1 alone suffices, because joint and bursal fluid appears bright on T1 and is therefore the most conspicuous sign of a tear.
Gradient-echo sequences alone are preferred, because their magnetic-susceptibility effects maximize conspicuity of cuff tears and fluid.

shoulder-m1-q3.explanation: On fluid-sensitive PD FS, fluid is bright and tracks through a full-thickness cuff defect into the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa, confirming a surface-reaching tear. Non-fat-suppressed PD and T1 give higher-SNR anatomic detail and show fatty muscle atrophy (Goutallier). Fluid is not bright on T1, and GRE blooming degrades cuff/fluid assessment.

shoulder-m1-q4.stem: A 58-year-old primary care sports medicine patient presents with 3 weeks of severe shoulder pain and global passive range-of-motion loss after a minor rotator cuff strain. You obtain a shoulder MRI and want to assess whether adhesive capsulitis findings support the clinical presentation. Which sequences and imaging findings are most useful for this question?

options.text: T1-weighted images alone, because they show anatomy best and are sufficient to rule out a tear.

Axial and coronal fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed sequences to visualize rotator interval edema, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and axillary pouch capsular thickening—findings that support adhesive capsulitis in the appropriate clinical setting and help guide injection or therapy planning.
Gradient-echo sequences are essential because adhesive capsulitis produces blooming artifact that proves the diagnosis.
Sagittal images are the only plane needed; axial and coronal images are redundant for assessing capsular inflammation.

shoulder-m1-q4.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis imaging findings—rotator interval edema, CHL thickening, axillary recess capsular thickening—are best seen on fluid-sensitive sequences and axial/coronal planes. MRI supports but does not replace the clinical diagnosis of global ROM loss; these imaging findings are management-relevant when they match the exam and guide injection-planning or exclusion of competing pathology.

shoulder-m2-q1.stem: After finding mild supraspinatus tendinosis, the reader stops and misses medial biceps subluxation from an upper-border subscapularis tear. What cognitive error does the search pattern help prevent?

options.text: Satisfaction of search
Recency bias
Lead-time bias
Verification bias

shoulder-m2-q1.explanation: Satisfaction of search occurs when finding one abnormality reduces vigilance for additional findings. The shoulder search pattern forces a complete review even after an obvious pain generator is found.

shoulder-m2-q2.stem: Which impression is most useful for a primary care sports medicine clinician?

options.text: Abnormal shoulder MRI.
Signal abnormality in the cuff and labrum.
Acute full-thickness supraspinatus tear with mild retraction and no advanced fatty atrophy; timely surgical referral is appropriate.
Degenerative changes are present.
